coredata.dc:title: State-of-the-art developments in carbon quantum dots (CQDs): Photo-catalysis, bio-imaging, and bio-sensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Mohammad Ehtisham
Mohammad, Akbar
Yoon, Taeho
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the intensifying nanostructured form of carbon material, have exhibited incredible impetus in several research fields such as bio-imaging, bio-sensing, drug delivery systems, optoelectronics, photovoltaics, and photocatalysis, thanks to their exceptional properties. The CQDs show extensive photonic and electronic properties, as well as their light-collecting, tunable photoluminescence, remarkable up-converted photoluminescence, and photo-induced transfer of electrons were widely studied. These properties have great advantages in a variety of visible-light-induced catalytic applications for the purpose of fully utilizing the energy from the solar spectrum. The major purpose of this review is to validate current improvements in the fabrication of CQDs, characteristics, and visible-light-induced catalytic applications, with a focus on CQDs multiple functions in photo-redox processes. We also examine the problems and future directions of CQD-based nanostructured materials in this growing research field, with an eye toward establishing a decisive role for CQDs in photocatalysis, bio-imaging, and bio-sensing applications that are enormously effective and stable over time. In the end, a look forward to future developments is presented, with a view to overcoming challenges and encouraging further research into this promising field.

head.ce:miscellaneous: Handling Editor: Dr Y Yeomin Yoon

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the intensifying nanostructured form of carbon material, have exhibited incredible impetus in several research fields such as bio-imaging, bio-sensing, drug delivery systems, optoelectronics, photovoltaics, and photocatalysis, thanks to their exceptional properties. The CQDs show extensive photonic and electronic properties, as well as their light-collecting, tunable photoluminescence, remarkable up-converted photoluminescence, and photo-induced transfer of electrons were widely studied. These properties have great advantages in a variety of visible-light-induced catalytic applications for the purpose of fully utilizing the energy from the solar spectrum. The major purpose of this review is to validate current improvements in the fabrication of CQDs, characteristics, and visible-light-induced catalytic applications, with a focus on CQDs multiple functions in photo-redox processes. We also examine the problems and future directions of CQD-based nanostructured materials in this growing research field, with an eye toward establishing a decisive role for CQDs in photocatalysis, bio-imaging, and bio-sensing applications that are enormously effective and stable over time. In the end, a look forward to future developments is presented, with a view to overcoming challenges and encouraging further research into this promising field.

Recent advances on carbon quantum Dots (CQDs) for multi-directional applications.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanostructured materials represent a wide-ranging category of materials that have emerged as promising candidates for novel materials, energy, and bio-engineering applications (Abbas et al., 2018; Chai et al., 2019; Long et al., 2021). The inimitable properties of carbon-based nanostructured materials, such as, nano-diamonds, carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, sheets of graphene, and fluorescent carbon particles or carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have been extensively used in research owing to their enormous potential for a diverse range of practical applications (Larson et al., 2003; Cao et al., 2007; Liu et al., 2009; Tuerhong et al., 2017). CQDs, also known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are a new-fangled type of carbon-based nanostructured material that fluoresces brightly (Liu et al., 2016). They are made up of unique, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with ultrafine particles that are less than 10 nm in size (Liu et al., 2016). The CQDs differ from antiquated semiconductor quantum dots or other carbon-based nanostructured materials (e.g., activated carbon, graphene, and carbon nanotubes) in that they combine the inimitable optical/photonic properties of quantum dots with the projecting electrical properties of sp2 carbon-based materials (Liu et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2016). The CQDs were discovered in 2004 during an investigation concerning the components of fluorescent nanoparticles, which were developed using carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) (Liu et al., 2016). In the early 1960s, these fluorescent nanoparticles were denoted as ‘‘carbon quantum dots’’ by (Sun et al., 2006) proposed a method for fabricating CQDs with significantly improved fluorescence emission via the surface interface. The fabrication of CQDs has led to extensive work on CQDs in various research fields, including bio-imaging, bio-sensing, drug delivery, photocatalysis, photovoltaics, and optoelectronics (Xu et al., 2004; Ray et al., 2009; Yang et al., 2009c; Antaris et al., 2013; Chen et al., 2013b; Hu et al., 2013a; Kleinauskas et al., 2013; Tang et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2013; Hou et al., 2015b; Fang et al., 2016; Wang and Qiu, 2016; Yu et al., 2016; Ji et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2020; Rahman et al., 2020, 2021; Shah et al., 2020).

CQDs are typically composed of a carbogenic core, with functional groups attached to surface sites (Guo et al., 2012). Additionally, most CQDs contain an amorphous to nano-crystalline core, predominantly composed of sp2 carbon, along with lattice spacing that is constant with graphitic carbon (Bourlinos et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2016a). In comparison to other carbon nanomaterials, CQDs are distinguished by the presence of oxygenic moieties at the surface, which render them highly soluble in aqueous solutions and enable additional functionalization (Yu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016a; Shah et al., 2020). In general, graphene-based quantum dots (GQDs) with comparable structures to CQDs may be viewed as a single class of CQDs, despite their high crystallinity (Yang et al., 2009b; Baker and Baker, 2010). As a result of the precise structures, CQDs exhibit diverse properties, including effective solar light absorption, adjustable photoluminescence, infrared-responded up-conversion of photoluminescence, and exclusive photoinduced electron transfer. A precise and selective fabrication route might alter the surface state of CQDs and enhance their tunable light emission, rendering them useful in a variety of photonic applications. Additionally, CQDs have been reported to initiate and exhibit exceptional upconverted photoluminescence, an optical phenomenon in specific, normal, as well as down-converted photoluminescence scenarios (Li et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2018). This trend may further indicate the possibility of using high-performance catalyst systems based on CQDs to efficiently consume the entire spectrum of sunlight. Furthermore, researchers have demonstrated that CQDs exhibit photoluminescence that can be induced by electron donor molecules or electron acceptor molecules in solution, proving that photoexcited CQDs can transfer electrons more efficiently than bare CQDs (Wang et al., 2009b).

Given their interesting photonic and electronic properties, it is unsurprising that CQDs can be used as photo-catalysts. The charge carrier's generation, that is, electrons and holes, by means of visible light, is a well-known photocatalytic process. However, several existing photocatalytic systems are limited in their effectiveness and applications in the real world due to their low utilization of visible light and fast recombination rate for photo-induced charge carriers (Wang et al., 2009b; Kwon et al., 2014; Yuan et al., 2014; Mousavi et al., 2018). As a result of CQDs' photoluminescence behavior and electron transfer properties, they are considered to be an essential component of high-performance photocatalyst fabrication (Khan et al., 2018). In addition to their role as electron mediators, CQDs can also function as photosensitizers, spectral converters, and sole photocatalysts. The CQDs are considered one of the most capable carbon nanomaterials in sensing research. Additionally, CQDs as a biosensor carrier are highly promising for bio-sensing applications due to their excellent photostability, biocompatibility, nontoxicity, ease of modification, and high solubility in water (Kim et al., 2010). Electrochemical sensors can precisely detect neurotransmitters (NTs) and are a valuable forensic investigation tool that are cost-effective, provide immediate results, and can concurrently detect two or more constituents (Wang et al., 2009a; Lai et al., 2018). The main advantages of electrochemical sensors are their high accuracy and repeatability in accumulation, their low detection limits, and their ability to provide real-time measurements (Liu et al., 2020b; Subhan and Muzibur Rahman, 2022). In addition, these types of sensors are suitable for on-site detection and can be used in targeted experiments for clinical and diagnostic purposes in conjunction with sturdy, moveable, or shrinkable devices (Kim et al., 2010; Han et al., 2018; Atta et al., 2019).

The urbanization of society has led to an increased focus on environmental issues. Global concerns have been raised over the consequences of damaging and toxic pollutants in the environment. The global scientific community is continuously working to reduce environmental pollution and protect human health (Cvitanovic and Hobday, 2018; Rahman et al., 2021). In recent years, the development of CQDs has also attracted great attention due to their unique physical chemistry properties, ultra-small size, good biocompatibility, and plentiful and inexpensive precursors (Wang and Hu, 2014; Sun and Lei, 2017; Devi et al., 2019; Park et al., 2020; Shah et al., 2021; Hayat et al., 2022). The following are some of the reasons that CQDs have significant environmental potential:
(i)
Their unique optical properties include strong fluorescent activity, excellent photostability, highly tunable photoluminescence, and excellent optoelectronic performance (Anwar et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020a)
(ii)
Due to the variety of materials and synthetic approaches used, CQDs possess a wide range of useful physicochemical properties, such as abundant surface functional groups, extremely small size, large surface area, excellent water dispersibility, great electrical conductivity, and great electron donor and acceptor properties (Yuan et al., 2016; Anwar et al., 2019).
(iii)
In addition to being inexpensive, CQDs are highly biocompatible and environmentally friendly (Lim et al., 2015). The peculiar properties of CQDs have allowed them to be used for monitoring and removing pollutants, as well as assembling water treatment membranes and providing an antibacterial material for pathogenic bacteria (Wang and Hu, 2014; Hou et al., 2015a; Lee et al., 2018; Hong et al., 2019)

Previously published reviews have described synthesis strategies and different applications of CQDs in bioanalysis, bioimaging, and energy conversion, which have greatly improved our understanding of CQDs and promoted their development (Petryayeva et al., 2013; Hu et al., 2019; Miao et al., 2020; Molaei, 2020). We believe that it is imperative and timely to provide insight into the most recent and significant advances in fabrication, their intriguing optical properties, their catalysis mechanisms, and their applications environmental remediation, sensing and bioimaging. These aspects have been illustrated in Fig. 1 . Additionally, the conclusion section discusses the major challenges, reasonable improvements, and potential future developments associated with CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development and fabrication progress of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In 2003, Xu et al. unintentionally discovered fluorescent CQDs while his group was cleaning single-walled carbon nanotubes using an arc discharge approach (Liu et al., 2016). Since then, a variety of synthetic approaches have been established for the fabrication of CQDs. Additionally, the research studies have achieved CQDs with progressive functions and a variety of compositions and structures using facile, cost-effective, and size-controllable approaches (Wang et al., 2019b; Nie et al., 2020). The facile fabrication of CQDs can be broadly classified into two groups, based on the direction of size progression of the materials used (Fig. 1): bottom-up and top-down. Intended for bottom-up approaches that are typically mediated via microwave-assisted synthesis, thermal decomposition, hydrothermal treatment, template-assisted routes, and plasma treatment, the fabrication of CQDs is investigated considering the fabrication of precursor molecules, such as, citric acid, glucose, and sucrose (Zhu et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2012, 2014; Chen et al., 2013a; Qu et al., 2013; De Benedetto et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2015b; Martindale et al., 2015; Kumar et al., 2018b; Terán-Alcocer et al., 2021). Conversely, top-down approaches make use of treatments such as arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation, and ultrasonics to fabricate CQDs from macroscopic carbons like activated carbon, graphite, and carbon nanotubes (Sun et al., 2006; Cao et al., 2007; Liu et al., 2009, 2016; Yang et al., 2009c; Jiang et al., 2010; Li et al., 2010b; Qiao et al., 2010; Zong et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2013a).

The hydrothermal carbonization has long been recognized as the most promising of all synthesis methods because it is highly efficient, affordable, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly (Qu et al., 2013; Bian et al., 2014; Mehta et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015). The hydrothermal approach utilizes heat and pressure to convert carbohydrates into high-value materials, fuels, and chemicals (Bian et al., 2014; Saha et al., 2019, 2020). A hydrothermal approach is usually performed at 180–260 °C for 5 min to 12 h under water saturation pressure, depending on the requirement of application (Berge et al., 2011). The water in these conditions exhibits a higher ionic product and a lower dielectric constant than ordinary water, making it a non-polar solvent (Bandura and Lvov, 2006). To create cross-linked polymeric materials, carbohydrates undergo hydrolysis, and the hydrolyzed products are then dehydrated, decarboxylated, condensation, and polymerized simultaneously (Sevilla and Fuertes, 2009b). In general, the particle sizes of the solid product that is commonly referred to as hydrohcar are between 10 nm and 100 μm (Sevilla and Fuertes, 2009a). As hydrothermal approaches are bottom-up, particles smaller than 10 nm remain in the liquid phase which is referred to as the process liquid.

However, the fabrication of CQDs poses certain difficulties:
(i)
To avoid the accumulation of carbonaceous particles during carbonization, electrochemical fabrication, confined pyrolysis, or solution chemistry can be utilized.
(ii)
For consistent properties and mechanistic studies, it is critical that CQDs have a controlled size and uniformity that can be adjusted using post-treatment methods, like as, gel electrophoresis, centrifugation, and dialysis processes.
(iii)
The solubility and selectivity of CQDs are related to surface properties, which can be adjusted during the fabrication process or post-treatment.
Thus, the characteristics of several synthetic approaches for the fabrication of CQDs are summarized in the next subsection (Table 1 ).

Microwaves are electromagnetic waves with a large wavelength ranging from 1 mm to 1 m, capable of delivering an excessive amount of energy to breakdown down the chemical bonding between surfaces (Zhang et al., 2019d; Hoang et al., 2021). In other words, microwave-assisted approaches may reduce the reaction time and provide instantaneous homogeneous heating, resulting in uniform quantum dot sizes (Li et al., 2012b; Park et al., 2014; Terán-Alcocer et al., 2021). Zhu et al. (2009), described the first microwave-assisted pyrolytic technique for fabricating CQDs with bright, constant luminescence and acceptable water dispersion that was both cost-effective and simple. During the fabrication process, different concentrations of polyethylene glycol (PEG-200) and saccharides were combined into distilled water to create a transparent solution (Fan et al., 2014). The transparent solution was then heated in a microwave oven (500 W) for 2–10 min. The color of the solution transformed progressively from colorless to yellowish, then turn into dark brown, suggesting the development of CQDs. Tang et al. (2012) employed a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique to produce glucose-derived water-soluble graphene-based QDs with precise size, which incorporates the advantages of both hydrothermal and microwave procedures (Tang et al., 2012). This method has several benefits, including ease of use, solvent-free synthesis, precursor tolerance, fast reaction times, low cost, and great scalability (Fig. 2 ).

In hydrothermal treatments, carbonization is considered to be a non-toxic, environmentally benign, and cost-effective method for extracting novel carbon-based materials from a diverse range of precursors such as saccharides, organic acids, juices, and fruit waste peels (Tang et al., 2012; Zhuo et al., 2012; Abd Rani et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). In general, carbon-producing mixtures are heated in organic solvents with a high boiling point, before being extracted and concentrated. Mohapatra et al. used a single-step hydrothermal treatment followed by centrifugation of orange juice to make highly photoluminescent CQDs with a quantum yield of 26% (Sahu et al., 2012). The manufactured CQDs, which range in size from 1.5 to 4.5 nm, are highly photostable and nontoxic, making them suitable for bioimaging (El-Shabasy et al., 2021). By hydrothermally processing chitosan at 180 °C for 12 h, Liu et al. showed the synthesis of fluorescent CQDs in a single step (Bhunia et al., 2013). For the fabrication of CQDs, carbonization followed by solvent extraction is a typical method (Bhunia et al., 2013). Carbon-yielding molecules were isolated and concentrated using organic solvents with high boiling points (Tajik et al., 2020). Studies conducted by Bhunia et al. further demonstrate the diameter of the hydrophilic and hydrophobic CQD probes, obtained using such approaches, to be less than 10 nm. To fabricate the hydrophobic CQDs, different amounts of carbohydrates were combined with octadecyl amine and octadecene and then heated to 70–300 °C for 10–30 min (Zhai et al., 2012; Tang et al., 2015). A wide range of pH values was used to heat an aqueous solution of carbohydrates. As an alternative, hydrophilic CQDs, with red and yellow emissions, can be produced by heating a solution of carbohydrates in phosphoric acid at 80–90 °C for 60 min. The resulting CQDs exhibit significantly improved biocompatibility, rendering them well suited for biomedical applications (Fig. 3 ).

Semiconductor and magnetic nanoscale materials have been created using thermal decomposition techniques (Wu et al., 2015). External heat adds to the carbonization and dehydration of organics, resulting in CQDs, according to recent studies (Xia et al., 2019). This thermal decomposition method is simple to operate, solvent-free, widely accepted as a precursor, requires less reaction time, and produces materials at a reasonable cost (Wang et al., 2012; Park et al., 2014; Kumar et al., 2018b).

Ma et al. (2015a), utilizes direct carbonization of ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA), which is heated in a sand bath at 260–280 °C, and it was possible to produce graphene-like structures on N-doped GQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , GQDs were grown using this fabrication strategy. As evidenced by the relatively high temperature at which graphitic carbon nitrides (g-C3N4) can be generated from N-containing compounds, a solid-state reaction could produce a graphite-like structure following EDTA decarboxylation. Martindale et al. (2015), used a simple pyrolysis of citric acid at 180 °C for 40 h to produce CQDs with a high yield of 45 percent. Furthermore, comparatively broad size distribution is observed with subsequent CQDs averaging 6.8 ± 2.3 nm in size.

A widely used approach for fabricating CQDs is laser ablation, which allows easy control of the CQD morphology, due to the variability of its nanostructures (Liu et al., 2009; Jiang et al., 2010; Zong et al., 2011; Rasal et al., 2021). (Sun et al., 2006) utilizes a laser to ablate graphite at 900 °C in the presence of water vapor in an argon flow to create CQDs. Furthermore, Li et al. used laser ablation to create CQDs employing nanoscale carbon/carbon-based materials as initial materials and liquid media (Li et al., 2010b). Typically, sonication was used to disperse 0.02 g of nanoscale carbon material added in 50 mL of solvent (like as, acetone, ethanol, or distilled water). Following the process of ultrasonication, 4 mL of the suspension was gently injected into a glass cell to allow laser light to pass through. To expose the suspension, a pulsed laser (Nd: YAG) with a second harmonic wavelength of 532 nm was used. The solution was centrifuged after being exposed to laser light to extract the CQD-containing supernatant. Hu et al. went on to show that the size of CQDs can be adjusted by changing the laser pulse width, which affects the growth and nucleation conditions (Hu et al., 2011). The laser's interaction with the graphite flake leads to the formation of a local high-pressure and high-temperature vapor/plasma plume surrounding the graphite flake's edge and the liquid medium around it. The laser creates a bubble through liquid confinement, which expands rapidly till it reaches the maximum size. As a result of the surrounding liquid's pressure, the bubbles shrink, causing the interior portion of the bubble to cool, resulting in clusters. Bubbles of various densities can possibly be created by regulating the laser pulse width, allowing the production of CQDs of various sizes (Fig. 5 ).

Among the technologies commonly used to develop CQDs, electrochemical oxidation offers the following advantages: high in purity, low in cost, high in yield, and controlled size with good reproducibility (Sahu et al., 2012; Bhunia et al., 2013; Mehta et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2017; Lai et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020b). However, very few studies have been stated so far that describe the electrochemical carbonization of small molecules into CQDs. Li et al., fabricated a size-controllable CQD using an alkali-assisted electrochemical procedure, which demonstrated benign optoelectronic properties (Xu et al., 2013). Zhou et al., also reported for the first time the electrochemical method for CQDs from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) (Zhou et al., 2007). They began by using two Pt sheets as support and working electrodes, in addition to a freely adjustable Luggin capillary as a reference electrode.

The electrolyte solution (alcohol) was turned into CQDs after electrochemical carbonization in a basic environment. As the potential for applications increases, the degree of graphitization and size of these CQDs increases as well. These CQDs with amorphous cores revealed excellent excitation characteristics, as well as size-dependent optical properties without requiring any elaborate passing or cleaning techniques. The quantum yields of these CQDs reached up to ∼15.9%. Additionally, their low toxicity, derived from small molecular alcohols used during the synthesis, is highly promising (Sahu et al., 2012; Shah et al., 2021) (Fig. 6 ).

The chemical oxidation method is effective and appropriate for achieving large scale fabrication, without complex and time-consuming processes (Cao et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2009c; Hu et al., 2011; Li et al., 2012a). Qiao et al., described a simplistic approach for the large-scale fabrication of CQDs with an excitation-wavelength (λex) and a dependent PL (Li et al., 2012a; Bhattacharya et al., 2015). The approach employs coal based activated carbon, wood based activated carbon, and coconut based activated carbon as the carbon bases, along with the addition of HNO3. The PL analysis of these CQDs revealed a high sensitivity to changes in pH values, which decreased in intensity as the pH increased from 2 to 12 (Mahat and Shamsudin, 2020). Furthermore, a reversible pH response was also observed for the PL mode. Additionally, these findings suggest that they could aid in monitoring cellular metabolism via proton release as proton sensors. As summarized in Table 1, various synthetic approaches from the groundwork of CQDs possess different features.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and properties of carbon quantum dots with respect to fabrication approaches

ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs are typically quasi-spherical in shaped and consist of either amorphous or crystalline carbon (Lu et al., 2009; Jorns and Pappas, 2021). Their principal components are sp2-graphitic carbon sheets (or) graphene and graphene oxide sheets structures combined with fluorescence-inducing sp3-chemically modified carbon sheets. It is worth noting that many investigators have validated the presence of crystalline sp2 carbon (Bhunia et al., 2013; Piazza et al., 2019). In comparison to GQDs, CQDs exhibit less crystallinity and contain more defects than GQDs, which generally have a crystalline structure similar to mono-layered or multi-layered graphene (Zhou et al., 2007). Despite their different core structures, CQDs and GQDs share a few oxygen-related functional groups (Larson et al., 2003). From this perspective, GQDs can be generally considered as a subset of CQDs (Zhou et al., 2007; Baker and Baker, 2010). Numerous models of the CQD core have been proposed in recent years, including a diamond-like structure, a graphite/graphite oxide structure, and an amorphous carbon structure (Li et al., 2012a; Hu et al., 2013b; Thangaraj et al., 2019). Through laser ablation, CQDs passivated with polyethylene glycol (PEG200 N) have produced diamond-like selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns (Larson et al., 2003). Bao et al., also reported CQDs with a lattice spacing of ca. 0.325 nm made with the (002) facet of graphite and generated via electrochemical etching utilizing carbon fibers (Bao et al., 2011). In addition, Zhu et al., reported CQDs that were highly photoluminescent and contained amorphous carbon particles and were generated using a hydrothermal procedure (Zhu et al., 2013). The surface functional groups of CQDs have been the subject of much research during the last few decades. According to Liu et al., soot from burning candles can be treated with oxidative acid to form luminous CQDs with OH and CO2H functional groups on their surfaces (Liu et al., 2007).

Molecular orbital (MO) theory has been proposed by several groups of researchers to prove the electronic structure of CQDs (Larson et al., 2003; Hu et al., 2013b; Park et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017). CQDs demonstrated n→π* and π→π* transitions in the majority of cases owing to their simply available transition energies. The aromatic sp2-hybridized carbons in the core of CQDs are responsible for their π-states (Larson et al., 2003; Hu et al., 2013b). According to Lee et al., the gap between π-states and aromatic rings decreases steadily with an increase in aromatic rings, just as it does for π-conjugation in organic molecules (Qu et al., 2012). CQDs are caused by lone pair-containing functional groups such as amine, carboxyl, amide, and thiol. When functional groups with electron lone pairs are attached to the aromatic sp2-hybridized carbons, electron transitions from their n states to the π*-states of the aromatic rings (n→π*) can occur (Wang et al., 2017).

Generally, CQDs prepared using a variability of methods contain a certain number of defects. Additionally, the Raman signal of CQDs produced electrochemically from graphite shows that they contain both disordered (D-band) and sp2 (G-band) carbon (Bourlinos et al., 2008; Hu et al., 2009; Liu et al., 2009).

For CQDs, except for the high intensity peak at 26°, which corresponds to the (002) planes (graphitic), the two new peaks appeared in XRD patter at 22.59° and 18.20° correspond to the amorphous carbon and hexagonal carbon atoms. Thus, it has been observed that electrochemical oxidation results in weak, widened graphitic peaks for CQDs, associated with pristine graphite. Additionally, the peak intensity increases due to the transformation from crystalline graphite to amorphous carbon, and partial transformation of sp2-hybrid carbon in the aromatic ring to sp3-hybrid carbon. Moreover, a striking increase in the ID/IG ratios was observed in the Raman spectrum after the electrochemical oxidation of graphite (Ming et al., 2012). The results show that during sp2 graphite oxidation, deoxygenated groups (C–O, CO, O–CO) develop at the carbon site, resulting in unoccupied lattice sites and sp3 carbon. The XPS and FTIR spectra (Ming et al., 2012). Further show that electrochemical oxidation produces oxy-functional groups and that sp2-hybridized carbon in the aromatic ring partially decomposes into oxygen functional groups (Bourlinos et al., 2013).

CQDs absorb light primarily in the ultraviolet (UV) range (260–320 nm), with a few absorptions extending to visible wavelengths. As a result, the p–p* transition of CC bonds, the n–p* transition of CO bonds, and/or others could be attributed to the absorption shoulders (Sun et al., 2006). CQDs produced by electrochemical oxidation of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have a small peak at 270 nm, with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 50 nm (Sun et al., 2006; Zhou et al., 2007). The as-obtained CQDs also exhibited analogous absorption patterns in the UV–visible range. An intriguing feature of these three CQDs was that they showed characteristic redshifts in absorption transitions, suggesting they had smaller electronic band-gaps than their precursors.

Recent studies have revealed that CQDs glow under certain conditions. The fabrication of nanoscale organic room temperature-dependent phosphorescent (RTDP) materials using water-soluble CQDs has been well accomplished, and their phosphorescence lifetime extended to second-order (∼380 ms) (Lupton, 2010; Zhang et al., 2011). When exposed to UV light at room temperature, the CQDs dispersed in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix showed clear phosphorescence. According to preliminary studies, the source of the phosphorescence may be ascribed to the aromatic carbonyls on to the surface of CQDs, present in the triplet state. Furthermore, in matrix embedded PVA molecules, the triplet excited state energy can be effectively protected from rotational or vibrational losses by hydrogen bonding these groups (Bao et al., 2011; Zhu et al., 2013).

The features of CQD chemiluminescence (CL) were first studied in conjunction with oxidants like as, potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and cerium (IV) (Lin et al., 2012). According to the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis, oxidants like KMnO4 and cerium (IV) can be utilized to inject holes into CQDs. The number of holes in CQDs is increased in this method, resulting in electron-hole annihilation and CL emission.

Additionally, CQD concentrations within a certain range had an effect on the CL intensity (Lin et al., 2012). Owing to the thermal equilibrium of electron distributions in CQDs, increasing the temperature enhances the CL, as illustrated in Figure. Additionally, CL properties may be altered by modifying the surface groups (Fig. 7 ) (Liu et al., 2007; Zhu et al., 2013). New CL phenomena were also beneficial for CQDs made in an alkaline solution (NaOH or KOH). In a solution of NaOH, the CQDs demonstrated excellent electron donor capability in the direction of dissolved oxygen, forming the superoxide anion radical (O2•‾). The findings, therefore, demonstrate the exceptional electron-donating capabilities of CQDs (Liu et al., 2007).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in photocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para: The growth of CQDs can be attributed to multiple promoting effects the nanoparticles have in a photocatalytic system, which leads to their great potential for the design of high-performance photocatalysts (Pirsaheb et al., 2018). The highly versatile CQD material can function as a mediator, spectral converter, adsorption center, photosensitizer, and sole photocatalyst (Wang et al., 2017). These multiple effects of CQDs tend to occur simultaneously in most cases. The functions performed by CQDs may vary, depending on where light is irradiated (Wang et al., 2017). CQDs show an electron-sink behavior in the ultraviolet (UV), which is useful for trapping scattered electrons produced by metal oxides. The excellent up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) and photosensitization properties of CQDs make them suitable for serving as Visible-NIR light harvesters and co-catalysts in the case of NIR radiation. In addition, CQDs can serve as electron acceptors that enable electrons to be removed from their donors (Wang et al., 2017; Tejwan et al., 2020). CQDs and bulk semiconductors have been combined to achieve successful tuning of optical properties of composite materials, as demonstrated by extended absorption edges to the visible region (Zhang et al., 2019c). When CQDs and semiconductors are coupled electronically during their conduction bands, a new carbon energy state is introduced between the π states of CQDs and the conduction bands of semiconductors. It is this newly formed carbon energy state that causes the tail to extend (Zhang et al., 2016b, 2019c).

The CQDs express a multitude of attributes that showcase their tremendous potential in the field of photocatalysis. In compared to other typical photocatalysts (TiO2, SnO2, CeO2, ZnO, and CdS), CQDs have an advantage of solubility in water, chemical stability, and low in toxicity (Khan et al., 2016, 2017; Wang et al., 2017; Mohammad et al., 2019, 2021; Khan, 2021). After the surface has been precisely adjusted, the CQDs exhibit exceptional optical properties of absorbance and photoluminescence (Farshbaf et al., 2018).

The high recombination rate of charge carriers is probably caused by random defects on the surface of the photocatalyst, which act as recombination centers for free electrons (Zhang et al., 2016b). An important factor that sustains the photocatalytic activity in the prepared photocatalyst is the efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes (Chen et al., 2020). However, in some cases, free charge carriers (holes and electrons) can be trapped by defects or oxygen vacancies, significantly increasing the probability of recombination (Bi et al., 2014; Park et al., 2016). Therefore, photocatalysis will be simplified by allowing photogenerated electrons and holes to be disposed of. The electron storage capacity of carbon nanostructures and carbon-based nanostructures has been well-established (Li et al., 2010a; Giorcelli and Bartoli, 2019; Chen et al., 2020). As a result, photoexcited electrons from semiconductors or other photocatalysts can possibly be shuttled through the CQDs' conducting network, interfering with photogenerated carrier recombination at their interface. Detailed studies have demonstrated that CQDs are easy to fabricate and have low toxicity when they are used as electron mediators (Ming et al., 2012; Puech et al., 2019; VA et al., 2021) (Fig. 8).

As wide band gap semiconductors have a poor ability to exploit solar light, narrow band gap metal oxides, such as hematite and bismuth molybdenum oxide, are often used in photodegradation reactions to enhance solar energy harvesting (Odobel and Pellegrin, 2013; Heng et al., 2021). Due to their low stability, high toxicity, and poor separation of charge carriers, their practical applicability is greatly limited. The low toxicity and high chemical stability of CQDs make them an excellent candidate for absorption of broad-spectrum light (Lu et al., 2016). The intrinsic down-conversion property of CQDs is not the only unique feature of this material, as they are also capable of UCPL, which enables conversion of long-wavelength excitation sources to short-wavelength light (Lu et al., 2016; Heng et al., 2021). Subsequently, the converted light carrying high photon energy can be absorbed by the nearby semiconductor. Therefore, an enhanced photo-response in semiconductors encourages more excited electrons to participate in photocatalysis (Zhao et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019a).

Over the past decade, the UCPL characteristic of CQDs has been extensively studied. The mechanism that drives this phenomenon, however, remains unclear. According to previous reports, UCPL is a multi-photon active process involving the absorption of two or more low energy photons followed by production of a high energy photon (Zhan et al., 2016; Badıllı et al., 2020). Shen et al. (2012), however, later opposed this theory, describing UCPL as an anti-Stokes transition, where the higher energy emission is due to the constant energy difference between the excitation and emission light (Shen et al., 2012). This phenomenon explained in Fig. 8, the π-electron that is excited to the LUMO π* orbital transits back to the HOMO σ orbital during vibrational energy relaxation. Those σ orbitals that are below the intermediate π orbitals produce excess emitted light energy because they are below the intermediate orbitals. In spite of the fact that CQDs emit short wavelengths, only a fraction of them can be harnessed during the excitation of semiconductors, depending on the absorption edge of the hybrid photocatalyst (Zhang et al., 2013).

The adsorption competence of CQDs towards the targeted organic pollutants also play a significant role in the photodegradation reaction. The CQDs with a high surface area to volume ratio could provide extra surface-active sites for pollutant interfacial adhesion as a nanoscale (Baba Makama et al., 2018; Heng et al., 2021) material. The adsorption mechanisms can be divided into two types: physisorption and chemisorption, with the interaction mode differing depending on the type of reactant species (molecules, atoms, ions) (Lu et al., 2016). The π–π stacking interaction between CQDs conjugated sp2 carbon structure and the aromatic rings of organic pollutants should improve their molecular interface in aqueous solution for organic adsorbates (Shen et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019a). The large number of functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, carboxyl, amino, and epoxy groups) present on the surface of CQDs would increase their interaction with a wide range of molecules and heavy metal ions, resulting in improved adsorption efficiency (Mehta et al., 2019; Ahmad et al., 2020). The free radicals can quickly oxidize the adsorbed pollutants on the CQDs surface, which increasing the overall rate of photodegradation.

Photosensitizers are now widely utilized to help with visible-light-driven photocatalysis by increasing the photo response spectrum of wide-band gap metal oxides (Yin et al., 2015; Wang and Lang, 2018). Photosensitization is a photo-excitation process that uses light-absorption molecules to cause chemical changes in the substrate molecules (Lu et al., 2016). Because of their excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and great availability, CQDs have been widely used as a promising light-harvesting photosensitizer throughout the last decade (Yue et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019a; Zhang et al., 2019b). The traditional photosensitizers such as organic dyes, metal complexes, and inorganic sensitizers have been replaced by CQDs since they are expensive, unstable, and highly poisonous, restricting their practical applications in aqueous solution (Baba Makama et al.; Wang et al., 2017; Heng et al., 2021). CQDs will capture low-energy photons to excite electrons from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) level to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level during the sensitization process (Jain et al., 2021). The charge transfer from the LUMO level of the sensitizer to the conduction band of the metal oxide substrate would be facilitated by the chemical connection between the heterostructures, making these host photocatalysts visible-light photoactive (Jain et al., 2021). The photocatalytic activity of ZnO sensitized with L-CQDs was 60% higher than that of bare ZnO, according to Liang et al. (2020), Bulk ZnO has essentially negligible light absorption at wavelengths larger than 500 nm, as shown in the UV–vis DRS investigation (Liang et al., 2020). The photo response range has been greatly increased to the perceivable region to the sensitization action of L-CQDs (up to 700 nm). Furthermore, the lowered band gap energy of 2.96 eV demonstrated that photons with energies larger than or equivalent to the band gap energy can excite L-CQDs. This indicated that the L-CQDs might be utilized as photosensitizers, increasing the bulk semiconductor's electronic density and results in improving photocatalytic activity.

Although CQDs are frequently found in combination with metal oxides to solve the issue of rapid charge recombination, their application as photocatalysts alone has shown tremendous promise in the degradation of pollutants. Under visible light, the photocatalytic activity of Fe2O3/CQDs composites for degrading hazardous gas greatly improves, according to Kang et al. (2007a), The electron-hole pairs react with the adsorbed oxidants/reducers (usually O2 and OH−) to form active oxygen radicals (e.g., ·O2- and ·OH), which have substantially stronger oxidation capacities and so aid in the breakdown of hazardous gases, as shown in Fig. 8. As a result of this process, CQDs serve as electron reservoirs that capture electrons generated by Fe2O3 or metal oxides when exposed to visible light, thereby inhibiting electron-hole pairing in Fe2O3/CQDs (Xu et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2021; Hayat et al., 2022).

CQDs have photoinduced electron transport and electron storage capabilities in addition to photoluminescence (PL) (Wang et al., 2017). As a result, combining CQDs with a suitable semiconductor allows the semiconductor to have a larger photo-absorption range while also enabling the separation of photo-induced charge carriers. CQDs have assisted the development of CQDs/semiconductor (or oxide) composites (CQDs/SiO2, CQDs/TiO2, CQDs/Cu2O, CQDs/C3N4) that display enhanced visible-light-induced photocatalytic performance owing to these features (Li et al., 2012b; Han et al., 2013; Yu et al., 2014; Feng et al., 2020).

Charge separation and transfer in CQDs/TiO2 composites are governed by a significant physical mechanism. As a result, the valence band maxima of TiO2 and graphene, respectively, are 7.88 and 5.16 eV below their vacuum energy. Furthermore, the significant diversity between these band orientations can be used to explain impulsive interfacial charge transfer. It might be possible for the photogenerated electrons in CQDs to be injected into the conduction band of TiO2, as well as their proper alignment within the band. The strong separation of charge between CQDs and TiO2 has been confirmed by measurements of photocurrent and photon-to-current efficiency (Fig. 9 ) as previously reported by other groups (Wang et al., 2013c; Yu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2017).

The size-dependent PL and excellent characteristics of UCPL were obtained by a simple, single-step alkali-assisted electrochemical synthesis of CQDs with diameters in the 1.2–3.8 nm range (Larson et al., 2003; Li et al., 2012a). To determine the up-conversion performance of CQDs, they are combined with catalytically efficient SiO2 and TiO2 in a composite system to comprehend the effective utilization of the complete visible spectrum. Since pristine TiO2 or CQD could not degrade methyl blue (MB), the degradation of MB caused by visible light confirmed that TiO2/CQD (SiO2/CQD) was able to degrade MB efficiently. The findings further confirmed that the exceptional photocatalytic performances of SiO2/CQDs and TiO2/CQDs could be ascribed to the interfacial interactions between CQDs and TiO2 (or SiO2) in these catalytic systems (Zhao et al., 2008; Wang et al., 2013b) (Fig. 10 ).

When exposed to visible light, CQDs with UCPL properties use a TiO2/CQD nanocomposite photocatalyst to discreetly transform visible light to shorter wavelength light (325–425 nm), which then activates TiO2 or SiO2 to generate electron-hole pairs (Larson et al., 2003; Hu et al., 2018; Molinari et al., 2020; Temerov et al., 2021). The photolysis reaction produces electrons and holes, which react with the adsorbed oxidants (typically oxygen) and reducers (commonly OH−) to form O2 − and °OH, respectively.

Following that, the MB pollutant continues to decompose (Larson et al., 2003; Kang et al., 2007a, 2007b). Furthermore, when a CQD is put onto the surface of TiO2/SiO2, electrons may be transported from the surface of TiO2 or SiO2 to CQDs owing to the coordinated band edge position. The charge separation and stabilization are enabled while the rate of recombination is slowed. As a result, a composite photocatalyst was specifically fabricated with CQD's conducting networks so that electrons can travel freely between the holes (Deng et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2013). Additionally, the holes with long lifetimes are held accountable for the enhanced photoactivity.

As a photocatalyst, CQD/ZnO nanocomposites were fabricated using a single-step hydrothermal process (Lin et al., 2012). A similar enhancement of photocatalytic performance was observed for the degradation of noxious gases, like as, benzene and methanol, when visible light is irradiated. This suggests that CQDs play a decisive role in the advanced photocatalytic activity of CQDs/ZnO nanocomposites (Shen et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019). In addition, the CQDs encumbered on the ZnO surface form a “dyade” structure, resulting in charge transfer when exposed to visible light (Yu et al., 2012). This system exhibits joint charge transfer states dominated by the CQDs, whereas leftward holes remain structurally and electronically close to the ZnO surface. The process ensures high responsiveness of photogenerated electrons and holes by preventing the rate of recombination of charge carriers. As a result, the electrons present in the conducting network of CQDs reacting with O2 are adsorbed on their surfaces and oxygen (•O2 −) radicals are produced (Yu et al., 2012) (Fig. 11 a).

CQDs may also transform long-wavelength light into short-wavelength light, which stimulates ZnO and produces electron-hole pairs (Fig. 11 b). Furthermore, benzene and the conjugated structure of CQDs have a π–π interaction, which means that benzene adsorption on CQD/ZnO nanocomposites has a lot of potential. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQDs/ZnO nanocomposites is due to the combination of these three beneficial properties (Zhang et al., 2016b).

According to the previous reports, CQDs/Cu2O covered with distended nanostructures are capable of harvesting near-infrared light, thereby enhancing their photocatalytic performance (Xu et al., 2013; Mohammad et al., 2021). The surface distended structures may be confirmed from the TEM and SEM images of a single CQDs/Cu2O particles (Li et al., 2012b). Furthermore, the CQDs/Cu2O composite HR-TEM image indicates a completely crystalline structure with the CQDs embedded in the Cu2O. Furthermore, the elemental mapping utilizes energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) indicates that CQDs are continuously deposited on to Cu2O surface (Li et al., 2012b). The CQDs/Cu2O composite has improved (near) infrared photocatalytic performance for dye degradation, which is due to the cooperative effect attributed to the Cu2O distended structures' high-light reflecting competence and the CQDs' UCPL behavior. As a result, CQDs/Cu2O photocatalyst irradiated with light (near infrared) demonstrates multiple reflective actions between its vacant spaces, resulting in a more efficient use of the source light and enhanced photocatalytic reactions (Mohammad et al., 2021).

CQD may convert (N)IR light (>700 nm) into shorter wavelength light (390–564 nm) that excites Cu2O and produces electron-hole pairs as a result of up-conversion. In response to the adsorbed oxidants (usually O2) and reducers (usually OH−), the electrons and holes produce radicals, such as, •O2 and •OH, respectively, termed as active oxygen radicals capable of degrading organic pollutants. The band edge's comparable location in the CQDs/Cu2O composite also means that electrons can be transferred from Cu2O to CQDs, improving charge separation and stability while limiting charge recombination efficiency. These composite materials have a greater photocatalytic performance due to the CQD network transporting electrons freely and the long-lasting holes on the Cu2O(Li et al., 2012b).
The performance of CQD-modified metal oxides as water disinfectants (TiO2, ZnO, Fe2O3 and Bi2MoO6 is reviewed in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in bio-imaging and bio-sensing

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs possess unique properties that make them potentially useful as sensors for ions, small molecules, macromolecules, as well as cells and bacteria. One of the most fascinating applications of CQDs is chemical sensing (Li et al., 2012b). The identification of heavy metal ions, like as, Hg2+, is critical because they have a negative impact on the atmosphere and human well-being. The CQDs were chosen for sensing of chemicals because of their low in toxicity, water solubility, strong photostability, and chemical stability.

The CQDs were exploited for the selective and precise detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solutions in the early days of chemical sensing (Guo et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2013; Sun et al., 2013). The fluorescence emissions of both CQD in solution and CQD immobilized in sol-gel are sensitive to the presence of Hg2+ ions, according to Goncalves et al. (Gonçalves et al., 2010), Laser-abated and NH2-PEG200, as well as N-acetyl-l-cysteine-passivated CQDs, were utilizes as fluorescent probes in this precise examination. Micromolar Hg2+ with a Stern–Volmer constant of 1.3 × 105 M−1 was shown to be capable of satiating the intensity of fluorescing CQDs (Lu et al., 2002; Barman and Sadhukhan, 2012).

According to the unusually large Stern-Volmer constant (Lu et al., 2002), the quenching mediated by Hg2+ appears to be the outcome of the development of a non-fluorescent stable complex between CQD and Hg2+. Substituting N-CQDs for laser-ablated CQDs resulted in a significant increase in sensitivity to nanomolar levels. Furthermore, static quenching is anticipated to account for fluorescence extinction, with a Stern-Volmer constant much larger than the prior system of 1.2 × 107 M-1 (Sun et al., 2013). The nitrogen element contained within the N-CQDs, potentially via –CN groups on the N-CQD surface, is credited with greatly increased Hg2+ sensing ability.

In addition to the sensing applications, CQDs have other uses in the chemical sensing of Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+ Cr (VI) and Ag+ (Guo et al., 2019; Sim et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Zeng et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019c; Qin et al., 2020; Wongso et al., 2020; Zhong et al., 2020; Boonruang et al., 2021). The fluorescence extinction induced by metal ions, as indicated above, is used in most operations, including Hg2+ detection. Using CQDs customized with lysine and bovine serum albumin (CQDs-BSA-Lys), Liu et al. established a method for selective Cu2+ identification in aqueous samples, such as, tap water (Liu et al., 2012a). The coordination reaction between Cu2+ and the –COOH and –NH2 groups of the CQDs was effectively used to detect Cu2+ with these CQDs-BSA-Lys-CQDs. In addition to the development of sensitive metal ion assays, CQDs have also found niche applications in the recognition of pH (Choi et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2019), C2O4 2−, PO4 3−, CN−, F−, S2−, ClO−, I− and NO2 gas (Li et al., 2012b; Bozetine et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2018a; Mehta et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019; Behnood and Sodeifian, 2020). Unlike metal ion assays, which allow for fluorescence quenching by altering it, many anion assays need quenching of complexes in CQDs. For example, the fluorescence of the CQDs was increased in the I- assay owing to the progress of more constant developments between the I- and metal ions, transferring the CQDs in the CQD-metal complexes (Du et al., 2013).

To date, a wide range of measurement variability and an abundance of preliminary resources have been used in the homework of CQDs for the evaluates, resulting in significant batch-to-batch and lab-to-lab variation. Earlier reports strongly suggest that fluorescence properties heavily depend on the composition of CQDs and the remaining chemical groups on to their surfaces. Thus, altering the initial constituents or synthesis process will certainly yield CQDs with different optical and physicochemical properties. Therefore, the groundwork for CQDs and the valuation of CQD performance must be adjusted.

In research and in clinical applications, bio-imaging plays an increasingly important role in observing and understanding biological processes at all levels, from the subcellular to small animal. Bioimaging probes were used to detect the first signs of the disease and monitor the behavior response (Zhao et al., 2017). CQDs have also been proposed for application in probing biological systems and detecting disease (Cao et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2009a). Furthermore, the observation that CQDs display PL in the near-infrared (NIR) spectral range in response to NIR light activation is significant. Because body tissues offer great transparency in the NIR “water window” NIR PL emission of CQDs triggered by NIR stimulation is precisely substantial and serious for in vivo imaging and has important potential in bio-nanotechnology (Sun et al., 2006). The fact that the PL found in CQDs may be satisfactorily met by either electron donor or acceptor molecules in solution is a noteworthy result, signifying that photoexcited CQDs are exceptional electron donors and acceptors. CQDs can be employed for different reasons in light energy conversion, photovoltaics, and related disciplines in addition to their interesting photoinduced electron transport capabilities (Wang et al., 2009b; Zhou et al., 2012b). According to recent studies, CQDs are a class of fluorescent nanomaterials with unique optical properties that can be used for bioimaging to diagnose and treat diseases in biological systems. These benefits include their low toxicity, high solubility, biocompatibility, and chemical inertness. In these circumstances, CQDs have become the most desirable material (Volkov, 2015; Liu et al., 2020a; Ansari et al., 2021). Liu et al. investigated the probability of employing CQDs as a fluorescence contrast agent in mice in a study (Tao et al., 2012). Mice were injected subcutaneously with PEGylated CQDs in an aqueous solution, and fluorescence pictures were taken at various wavelengths of excitation during the studies. It was discovered that the contrast between green and red emission was sufficient for clear imaging (Yang et al., 2009a).

Tao et al. used the same approach on stripped mice and got similar findings as presented in Fig. 12 (Tao et al., 2012). Mice were injected subcutaneously with an aqueous solution of CQDs, then fluorescence imaging was performed utilizing excitations at seven distinct wavelengths ranging from 455 nm to 704 nm (Tao et al., 2012). Additionally, the 595 nm excitation wavelength produced the best contrast in fluorescence (Ding et al., 2016). It is evident that multiple imaging technologies should be integrated into a single agent for comprehensive disease assessment. In nanostructured multimodal imaging probes, optical imaging modalities and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) are most typically coupled. MRI offers spatial resolution and the ability to attain anatomical and physiological evidence quickly, whereas optical imaging permits for fast screening (Estelrich et al., 2015). Zboril et al., described the utilization of an organic salt and a gadolinium (III) complex to synthesis ultrafine Gad (III)-doped CQDs with fluorescence and MRI properties in recent research (Bourlinos et al., 2012).

Water dispersibility was observed, as well as appropriate visible light excitation fluorescence and T1-weighted MRI contrast similar to commercial Gadovist; the dots also showed low cytotoxicity. The findings suggest that combining a common T1-MRI agent with CQDs via strong covalent bonding could significantly boost the rotational correlation time (τR) of T1-imaging probes (Gong et al., 2011; Chen et al., 2013c). Iron oxide-doped CQDs (IO-CQDs) were fabricated by Srivastava et al. for multi-modal (MR/fluorescence) bio-imaging (Srivastava et al., 2012). IO-CQDs were made via thermal breakdown of organic precursors in the presence of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The fluorescence exhibited largely in the cell cytoplasm suggests that the IO-CQDs are taken up by RAW 264.7 cells. In vivo imaging was performed after injecting rats intravenously with the IO-CQDs, wherein the IO-CQDs in the spleen slide samples emitted fluorescence signals. Furthermore, both the T1 and T2 models confirmed the presence of enhanced signals in the brain blood vessels. Thus, due to their biocompatibility, CQDs can also be used in conjunction with other imaging technologies for multi-modal bio-imaging (Muthulingam et al., 2016). Though the exact mechanism of CQD acceptance by cells is not clear, increasing experimental evidence suggests that CQD might be incorporated into cells through endocytosis without significant nucleus infiltration (Cao et al., 2007; Bourlinos et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2015b). Furthermore, the interface between the protective and functional coatings on CQDs is thought to be significant in determining cell targeting. Thus, by conjugating CQDs with enabling proteins or peptides, it may be able to improve CQD targeting by allowing CQDs to pass through the cell membrane more easily.

Summary of a few CQDs characteristics for examining nano-carrier actions in biological systems:
(i) Small size: Quantum dots whose core size is only 2–10 nm permits loading numerous drug carriers while minimizing side effects on the carriers.
(ii) Multipurpose surface chemistry: Surface-coating procedures compatible with several coating techniques.
(iii) Tapered emission profile: The recognition of singular quantum dots relies on their distinctive emission peaks.
(iv) Excellent brightness: Singular quantum dots can be detected within a specimen because of their excellent brightness
(v) Excellent photostability: Quantum dot tracking is sustained by photo bleaching resistance.
(vi) Sizeable Stokes shift: A wide gap between excitation wavelength and the emission peak is responsible for a lessening in the influence of auto-fluorescence
(vii) High sensitivity to microenvironment: Quantum dots fluoresces when exposed to pH, surface passivation, and drugs, resulting in micro-environment sensing
(vii) Electron-dense inorganic core: Specific localization of nano-particle movement is possible within cells with a high quantum dot contrast.

Biosensing has entered the era of nanoprobes recently, in which diseases can be diagnosed more effectively and promptly at the molecular and cellular level due to the rapid growth of nanotechnology (Liu et al., 2019). Nanotechnology and biosensing are combined with great advantages over traditional methods, including high-throughput screening, enhanced detection limits, real-time analysis, label-free detection, and substantially reduced sample sizes (Chauhan et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018).

Because of its solubility in water, flexibility in surface modification, non-toxicity, multicolor excitation dependent emission, excellent bio-compatibility, and high photosensitivity, CQDs have been effectively used as biosensors. CQD-based biosensors may measure a variety of factors in addition to glucose, including copper, iron, phosphate, potassium, pH, and nucleic acid (Li et al., 2011b; Shi et al., 2011, 2012; Wei et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012, 2013). The CQDs can also be used to identify single-base mismatches in nucleic acids with high and precise sensitivity and selectivity. In summary, single-stranded fluorescent DNA (ssDNA) (Li et al., 2011b) probes were adsorbed by CQD through π– π interactions, as well as quenched fluorescence and directly hybridized to their targets, which accompanied double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) [176]. The antibodies predict the discriminating tags, and the CQDs produce fluorescent signals in this overall nucleic acid LFA (NALFIA) (Sukhanova and Nabiev, 2008; Posthuma-Trumpie et al., 2012)example (Fig. 13 ). To identify the persistent organic pollutant 4,4′-dibrominated biphenyl (PBB15), Bu and colleagues (Bu et al., 2014) developed an immunosensor based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and homogeneous immunoassay (Li et al., 2011b). Anti-PBB15 antibodies with gold nanoparticles (AuNP) functionalization and CQD-labeled PBB15 antigens serve as fluorescence acceptors and donors, respectively, in the immunosensor. As a result, the AuNPs effectively muted the CQDs' fluorescence via FRET. Due to competitive immunoreaction, CQD-labeled antigens were liberated from AuNP surfaces in the presence of PBB15, and the fluorescence was regained. It can be used as a model for developing immunoassays with appropriate antibodies and antigens for detecting other analytes in the future.

Aptamers, target binders chosen from a huge database of nucleic acids, were used in another application of CQDs (Posthuma-Trumpie et al., 2012; Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013). Aptamers have a lot of promise in terms of permitting detectable changes in radiometric response to their targets due to conformational changes (Khung and Narducci, 2013). By thrombin-aptamer interactions, Xu's group (Xu et al., 2012) confirmed that thrombin causes aptamers-functionalized CQDs to form a sandwich-structure with aptamers-functionalized silica nanoparticles. The detection limit of one of the more sensitive fluorescence tests for thrombin was 1.0 nm, and it was highly selective. Fluorescent CQDs made by microwave pyrolysis were utilized as markers for protein identification in gel electrophoresis in this study (Na et al., 2013). The CQDs have demonstrated comparable or even better sensitivity for staining proteins than traditional staining agents, like as, Coomassie Brilliant Blue and Ag+. Additionally, water-soluble CQDs generated by thermally combusting rice straws under low airflow were used to detect and count bacteria in wastewater (Mandal and Parvin, 2011).

Optical Biosensing of CQDs; The CQDs have exceptional optical properties and release in both visible light and infrared regions, thus it can be commonly utilizing in optical biosensing (Liu et al., 2019). The PL of CQDs is primarily influenced by their sizes and surface area (Zhu et al., 2015; Cayuela et al., 2016). Subsequently the interface of CQDs with analytes possibly will be adjust the surface states and the properties of PL, like as, intensity, emission wavelength, etc. Of CQDs possibly will be simply influenced (Yan et al., 2019). The degree of PL modification, like as, fluorescence quenching of CQDs is then utilize to sense and examine the target analytes in a typical CQDs-based optical biosensor (Nazri et al., 2021). Shen et al.(Shen and Xia, 2014) technologically advanced a glucose biosensor from phenylboronic acid-derived CQDs; owing to the interface from the surface of boric acid of CQDs with glucose, FRET energy transfer occurs between CQDs and glucose which effectively quenching the fluorescence of CQDs (Ji et al., 2020). The method of quenching fluorescence is then used for monitoring glucose levels in the body which achieves a sensitivity of 10–250 times greater than the old boric acid fluorescent nano-sensing system (Kumar et al., 2022). A similar approach has also been practical for the sensing of vitamin B12 (Yu et al., 2019). General, fabrication of biosensors straight from CQDs will reduce complexity by avoiding tedious superficial apposition or bioreceptor loading, which could be of significant benefit in future large-scale construction and applications. Examples of CQDs for biosensors in which external bioreceptors are not required are summarized in Table 3.

The CQDs are in unique fluorescent nanoparticles with a benign composition and good biocompatibility, bioimaging and biosensing would undoubtedly be their primary technical applications. As a result, CQDs perform exceptionally well in bioimaging and biosensing applications. The exemplary instances of synthetic approaches created for the synthesis of CQDs are summarized in Table 4.

The unique and novel properties and advantages of CQDs have recently attracted a lot of attention due to their close relationship to their structural defects and quantum confinement effects (Table 4 ). Conventionally, CQDs have been characterized as quasispheroidal, discrete nanoparticles with a size below 10 nm (Bourlinos et al., 2008). The unique properties and advantages of the CQDs have inspired extensive research, especially for the applications in which the labels must be inexpensive, smaller in size, and biocompatible (Cao et al., 2007; Liu et al., 2007; Bourlinos et al., 2008). The CQDs' structural advantages, their surface functional groups, and their high surface defect density made it possible to use them as a and effective photocatalysts with several different bio-imaging and bio-sensing applications. Additional advantages of CQDs over organic dyes and traditional semiconductor quantum dots are low cost and low toxicity, facile functionalization, good biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence emissions, and resistance to photobleaching (Ray et al., 2009; Li et al., 2011a; Luk et al., 2012). As a result, CQDs are considered as a potential replacement for semiconductor quantum dots in photocatalysis, bioimaging, biological labeling, and drug delivery systems (Cao et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2009a, 2009b; Pan et al., 2012).

The CQDs are an emerging class of fluorescent nanomaterials with tremendous potential for numerous applications including bio-imaging, photodynamic therapy, chemistry sensing, bio-sensing, drug delivery, electrocatalysis, and photocatalysis (Liu et al., 2007; Shankar et al., 2020). Photocatalysis has attracted increasing research attention to CQDs. It is well known that photocatalysis generates a charge carrier, such as, electrons and holes owing to the action of visible/UV lights (Zhang et al., 2016b). Thus, the ideal photocatalyst ought to be able to absorb a wide variety of photogenerated charge carriers simultaneously and have high separation efficiency. There are many current photocatalytic systems which suffer from low sunlight penetration and high recombination rates of the photoinduced charge carriers, which severely limit the overall quantum efficiency (Kamat, 2008). To improve the separation efficiency and to increase the spectral responsive range, it is necessary to develop effective ways to improve charge separation efficiency (Jiang et al., 2017). The CQDs will be effective response towards photocatalytic, bio-imaging and bio-sensing applications.

This section ends with the message that the way in which a scientist approaches for the fabrication of CQDs and applied for the photocatalysis has multiple characteristics and is dependent on his or her interests and competencies. The benefits of photocatalysis to the overall community are evident in all cases but also depend on the rigor and quality of the experiments. We finally believe that incorporating multipurpose applications of CQDs into the design of advanced photocatalytic materials is a significant contribution to both the photocatalysis community and biomedical research (accurate imaging and sensing).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future perspectives for carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs have attracted a lot of consideration in the scientific community since their discovery because of their versatility in binding to various bulk semiconductors for a wide range of solar energy conversion applications. CQDs have demonstrated to be highly relevant to various applications ranging from energy storage to bioanalysis. Further, this review highlights the variety of carbon resources used in CQD fabrication, including conventional chemicals and sustainable raw materials. Despite this enormous potential, assembling CQDs that have high efficiency and quality continues to remain challenging. As a result, the current review focused on the fabrication method for CQDs-based semiconductor photocatalysts and highlighted their recent advancement in waste-water treatment. Additionally, it assesses their ability to degrade a variety of environmental pollutants. Given that green synthetic routes for CQDs are still in their infancy in comparison to chemical-derived CQDs, the current review emphasizes the importance of researchers shifting their focus to more environmentally friendly options. We have also presented a comprehensive overview of CQDs used in photocatalysis in the review. Furthermore, improving the sensitivity, selectivity, and resilience of sensing and bio-imaging platforms is critical, as well as that of applying delayed fluorescence techniques to reduce the interference caused by auto-fluorescence of biological samples in bio-imaging and bio-sensing applications based on CQDs, have been discussed. Importantly, the CQDs stability is also crucial parameters thus need to be considered for future applications.

While CQDs-based photocatalysts may be developed with sophisticated designs and superior performance, yet there are still several challenges in this area that need a stable solution. To advance the technological development of CQD-based composites for photocatalytic degradation, the following issues must be addressed:
(i) Hydrothermal treatments have been extensively utilizing in the synthesis of high-quality CQD-based photocatalysts because they involve simplistic treatment steps that do not necessitate the use of harsh acids or severe working conditions. However, the lengthy synthesis process (8–12 h) frequently results in considerable energy usage, making mass production economically and commercially undesirable. So, the developing a reliable synthesis method for CQDs at a large scale remains crucial.
(ii) Although several CQDs have been synthesized successfully with high-quality yields, their complex internal structure and photoluminescence mechanism remain unknown. Additionally, no conclusive evidence has been found to reveal the UCPL effects of CQDs. To understand how the optical properties of CQDs can be tuned, a thorough experimental and theoretical investigation is required, emphasizing crystallinity, size, morphology, & surface-defects.
(iii) Previous experiments have demonstrated that CQDs with varying sizes, shapes, and surface functionality exhibit altered PL properties. Although the CQD surface defects are sized, shaped, and occupied in a random manner, no well-defined methods, or techniques to achieve accurate control of these parameters currently exist. As a result, the advancement of consistent synthesis methods for improved control of CQD properties is becoming increasingly important in all applications.
(iv) While CQDs are well-known for their role in photocatalytic activities in composite systems, their direct usage as a photocatalyst has gotten less attention and remains unexplored. Because of their narrow band gap between HOMO and LUMO levels, CQDs have been shown to be capable of serving as solitary photocatalysts in several recent investigations. Additional fundamental investigations and extensive analysis are needed to fully exploit the potential of CQDs in photocatalytic processes.

In addition to the above recommendations, future studies should focus on the synthesis of highly effective green photocatalysts. Despite current challenges and concerns, CQDs have the potential for rapid technological advancement in multiple spheres of application. Researchers in the fields of bio-imaging and bio-sensing are becoming aware of the advantages of CQDs, and this development will continue in a synergistic relationship with adjacent research areas. It is, therefore, evident that CQDs hold a significant promise for the future.

While CQDs are still in the early stages of research, they have already shown tremendous promise in nanotechnology for assays, sensors, bio-imaging agents, drug movers, phototherapy, and photocatalysis. Even though many of the optical and electronic features of CQDs remain unknown, they have the more potential to make substantial contributions to bio-imaging and bio-medical research in the near future if further research is conducted. The high degree of adaptability of CQDs also enables them to be rationally modified in response to changing circumstances. Additionally, the application of CQDs in unexpected fields such as photocatalysis has been well demonstrated. Consequently, researchers have begun to shift their research attention from traditional fields, towards more imperative and insistent issues, like as, clean, and renewable energy. The CQD research is also attracting interest from researchers in a range of domains, including materials engineering, synthetic chemistry, nanomedicine, drug delivery, and clean-renewable energy. Evidently, research on CQDs continues to grow in synergy with adjacent fields, thereby suggestive of an optimistic future for CQDs.
Mohammad Ehtisham Khan: Writing-original draft, Review & Editing. Akbar Mohammad: Conceptualization, Writing-Review & Editing. Taeho Yoon: Resources, and Funding acquisition.
